The doctrine that the apostle Paul wrote about explained how the promises made by prophets were fulfilled. It spoke of God’s Son, Jesus the Savior, the promised Messiah, who came from David in his human nature, but was also declared to be the Son of God by the divine power that raised him from the dead. The Christian profession does not consist of a mental understanding or a simple agreement, much less in arguing against truth, but in obedience. And only those who are effectively called by Jesus Christ are brought to obedience of the faith. Here is 1. The privilege of Christians: they are beloved of God and are members of that body which is loved. 2. The duty of Christians: to be holy; this is what they are called to, called to be saints. These people, the apostle greeted them by wishing them grace to purify their souls and peace to comfort their hearts, as it comes from the free mercy of God, the reconciled Father of all believers, and through the Lord Jesus Christ.

We must show love for our friends not only by praying for them but also by praising God for them. In our plans and desires, we must remember to say, “If the Lord wills.” Our journeys are made prosperous or otherwise according to the will of God. We should readily share with others what God has entrusted to us, rejoicing in making others joyful, especially enjoying fellowship with those who believe the same things as we do. If redeemed by the blood and converted by the grace of the Lord Jesus, we belong to him completely; and for his sake, we are indebted to all people to do all the good we can. Such services are our duty.

In these verses, the apostle explains the purpose of the entire letter, in which he presents a charge of sinfulness against all humanity; declares the only way to be delivered from condemnation by faith in God’s mercy through Jesus Christ; and then builds upon that purity of heart, grateful obedience, and earnest desires to improve in all those Christian qualities and attitudes, which nothing but a lively faith in Christ can produce. God is a just and holy God, and we are guilty sinners. It is necessary for us to have righteousness to present before him: there is such a righteousness brought in by the Messiah and made known in the gospel; a gracious way of acceptance despite our guilt. It is the righteousness of Christ, who is God, coming from a satisfaction of infinite value. Faith is all-important, both at the beginning and throughout Christian life. It is not from faith to works, as if faith puts us into a justified state, and then works keep us in it; but it is always from faith to faith; it is faith pressing forward and gaining victory over unbelief.

The apostle begins to show that all people need the salvation of the gospel because no one could obtain God’s favor or escape his wrath by their own actions. For no person can claim that they have fulfilled all their obligations to God and to their neighbor; nor can anyone truly say that they have fully acted according to the light provided for them. The sinfulness of people is described as ungodliness against the laws of the first table, and unrighteousness against those of the second. The cause of that sinfulness is holding the truth in unrighteousness. Everyone, more or less, does what they know to be wrong and omits what they know to be right, so no one can claim ignorance. Our Creator’s invisible power and Godhead are clearly shown in the works he has made, so even idolaters and wicked Gentiles are left without excuse. They foolishly followed idolatry; and rational creatures changed the worship of the glorious Creator for that of animals, reptiles, and senseless images. They wandered from God until all traces of true religion would have been lost if the revelation of the gospel had not prevented it. For whatever may be claimed about human reason’s ability to discover divine truth and moral obligation or to govern practice correctly, facts cannot be denied. And these plainly show that people have dishonored God with absurd idolatries and superstitions; and have degraded themselves with vile desires and abominable deeds.

In the extreme wickedness of the Gentiles, the truth of our Lord’s words was shown: “Light has come into the world, but people loved darkness rather than light, because their actions were evil; for whoever does evil hates the light.” The truth was not to their liking. And we all know how quickly a person will try to reason themselves out of believing what they dislike, even against strong evidence. But a person cannot be brought to greater slavery than to be given over to their own desires. As the Gentiles did not like to keep God in their knowledge, they committed crimes wholly against reason and their own welfare. The nature of people, whether pagan or Christian, is still the same; and the apostle’s charges apply more or less to the state and character of people at all times until they are brought to full submission to the faith of Christ and renewed by divine power. There has never been a person who did not have reason to lament their strong corruptions and their secret dislike for God's will. Therefore, this chapter is a call to self-examination, the end of which should be a deep conviction of sin and the necessity of deliverance from a state of condemnation.
